speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four. Today is January 2, 1997. The survivor being interviewed is Janet Singer Applefield. The interviewer is Beth Cohen. The interview is being conducted in Plymouth, Massachusetts, in the United States of America. The language of the interview is English. Janet, you were talking about seeing your father again after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wasn't that comfortable with my father, because I didn't remember him. It had been-- I'd been separated from him-- well, over two years-- more than two years. And-- and I-- I always felt so uncomfortable with that feeling. I said, how could I not be excited to see my father, you know-- my father survive. And I never understood that till I heard another survivor say a very similar thing, But anyway, my father was very ill. And he needed time to recuperate. So he-- he rented a room nearby and got stronger, and stayed there for maybe a month. And-- and then he came and took me away. We went back to Nowy Targ. Several other people returned. And I'd say there were about 10 of us. I was the only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: But do you mean out of the family--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or out of the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of the Jewish community who returned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we all went back and lived in my grandfather's-- above the store. There was an apartment. The apartment apparently was all burnt out. I think that when the Russians came, they-- they-- they camped. They actually camped there. You know, they lit a fire and stuff. So we-- we lived there. My father and a couple of his friends started a little business-- once again, he had to earn a living. They started a candy business. And there was a woman, a friend. Her name was Hanka-- Hanya-- and she took care of me and cooked for us. But anti-Semitism was raging. And several of my father's friends were murdered after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were traveling. They were driving a car, and they were just murdered on the road. And we started to get notices posted on the door that we would be killed if we didn't get out of town, and that, you know, that Hitler didn't finish his work.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father went to the police chief and said, you know, what can you do? How can you help us? He said, the only thing I can do for you is to give you guns. So we had guns, and we slept with guns under our pillows.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, I guess, in time, we all realized that we couldn't stay there. We weren't safe. So we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who left?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all left, and people went in different-- different directions.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had you-- were you there in Nowy Targ before you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably not very long-- maybe months. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was at the end-- the late 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure when.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to Krakow. And my grandparents-- my grandfather, and my aunt, and uncle, the ones that ended up in Siberia-- returned. They survived. My grandmother died in Russia. And we all lived together in an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out what happened to your mother? Or at--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my father must have told me, and said your-- your mother died. Or I don't know how he told me. I don't know when he told me. I don't remember his words. I-- somehow I-- I was told, and I knew. I don't remember any reaction. I don't-- you know, I was so closed off, you know. And I just don't remember feeling anything of anything very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we were in Krakow. And then my father said, look. We-- we-- we can't stay here. We have to try to leave. And he said to me, where would you like to live-- in the United States or in-- or-- it wasn't Israel-- in Palestine? And I said, United States, because I had always heard things about United States-- one of the myths, you know-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --like money grows on trees, and things like that. My father had a brother in-- in Palestine. He was one of the you know, one of the settlers who left in 1935. He was one of the brothers that got away from my grandfather. [LAUGHS] He was very idealistic and started a kibbutz-- kibbutz Ein HamIfratz. And then another uncle who survived, my Uncle Jack, was-- he-- when the war began, he was in Italy studying engineering. And he escaped on a boat to Shanghai. He was taken prisoner of war by the Japanese. And he was in a prison during the war. And he returned to the United States. So I had an uncle in neither-- in-- in both countries. So we also had some distant cousins in the United States who sponsored us to come. And in March-- March 25, 1947, we-- well, first, we went to Paris. And we-- we-- we were there a couple months. My father enrolled me in-- in a school to learn English at that point. And then in March 25, 1947, we-- we came on a boat to New York. It was-- the boat's name was the Marine Falcon. It was a marine boat. You know, the women had to be on one floor, and the men were on a-- on another floor. So I was separated again from my father. And I think that--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the trip take?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took-- it took a long time. I think it took about 10 days or 8 days, something like that. There was a raging storm. And-- and then we came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: The two of you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The two of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anybody else with you on--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just the two of us. And we came on a visitor's visa, which allowed us to be in the United States for 90 days. At that point, you know, the only way we could remain if-- was if my father married an American. So the family kind of searched and looked for a single, eligible woman. And they found my stepmother. We came in March, and they were married in June. [LAUGHTER] And when we first came, we lived with my uncle and my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Rahway, New Jersey. And-- and my uncle and my-- he married an American woman-- my aunt Rose-- and her brother and sister, and we lived with them. My uncle took me-- so this was end of March. Well, actually, though, April, because by the time we arrived-- and like-- like toward-- I think in May-- they took me-- my uncle took me to school to enroll me in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- well, before that-- before that, there was this real attempt to make me an American child, you know? There was no assimilate-- there was assimilation rather than maintaining your own, you know, identity. And so my hair was cut-- cut short. And, you know, like my aunt took me for clothes. And-- and-- Did-- did you want to be more American? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember wanting. I was-- I was a child who was just so complacent, because I just did what I was told to do. It was like-- I don't remember rebelling, or wanting, or not wanting. It was like, look. This is what needs to happen, and it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think it was just so deeply ingrained that my-- my life depended on it, you know? So they just cut my hair, and I don't-- I don't think I was upset about it. You know, I was, like, OK. Do whatever you need to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so my-- my uncle took me to school. And as-- as he was enrolling me, he said, well, what name would you like to have? And he said, you know, you have to have an American name. You're an American girl now. So I picked the name Janet. And I think in the family there was a cousin. Her name was Janette. In Polish it's pronounced Janette. And I think I liked it. She was a very lovely woman-- looking-- good-looking woman. I said, I like Janet. So he enrolled me in school. And I started-- that was the end of the school year, but I was in-- put-- placed in a fifth/sixth grade combination. I was speaking very little English. And toward the end-- well, the end of the school year, I was promoted to my grade, which was seventh grade.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that point, you know, my father met my stepmother. She did not speak any-- any Polish, but they both spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never learned Yiddish. And they got married June 5th. My father didn't speak any English. And so he had to-- he was going to the movies every day to learn very-- you know, as fast as he could. He also got a job in Macy's, in the sporting goods department; learned English, got married. They bought a business.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business?

speaker: interviewee
text: A hardware store in Newark, New Jersey. For the first year, we lived with-- with my stepmother's mother, in Carteret, New Jersey. We lived there for a year. I went to school-- seventh grade. And I'd fit in. I was accepted. When I was-- when I got into the eighth grade, my eighth-grade teacher referred to me as the little foreign girl. She always sort of identified me that way. [LAUGHS] And so we moved to Newark. And my-- my stepmother, Sadie-- her-- her maiden name was Allman-- Sadie Allman-- she adopted me. And they worked very hard, very hard, in the store, you know, because they had to catch up. She was in her early 40s. And I guess my father was, too. So they had to make up for lost time. She had never been married before.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you grew up, then, in New Jersey?

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up in New Jersey, right. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- then what happened in-- in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents' ambition was to send me to college-- send me away. You know, I couldn't live at home-- I mean, not that I couldn't, but they thought I-- I should have that experience. So I did. I went to Syracuse, and-- you know, with nice clothes, and did very well; did well in high school, got-- got good grades-- just-- just adapted very well-- wrote for a newspaper. You know, I did a lot of the other things. But anyway, so then I went to college, and I-- I met my husband. I was still in high school. I was a senior in high school. He was a senior in college. And-- but I transferred from Syracuse to Boston University. And then I was here for a year, and we got engaged. And so I came back to New Jersey, and I finished at Rutgers. So that was my college career.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you study in college?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a history-sociology major. And-- but while I was still in school, I had my son, David, my oldest son. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in 1956. I was married in 1955. But I hadn't quite finished college. So-- but I-- I finished-- carried him to school--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --many times, held him. You know, I took my finals with him in my arm. And-- and then-- then I was home-- [LAUGHS] for-- you know, I didn't work after I graduated. I graduated in 1958. I had three kids. And in 1978, I went back to school for my graduate degree, at Boston University.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In social work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you working now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what are you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a clinical social worker, and I work with the geriatric population.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were a teenager in New Jersey, did any of the other kids ever ask you where you came from? Was anyone ever curious about what had happened to you as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. They knew that I was born in Poland, but I never talked about my experiences with them, because nobody really asked. The Holocaust wasn't really discussed very much in those days. I always talked about it with my family, with my kids. My father always talked with me, you know, he told me what happened to him. And I-- I was always very open with my children about it. So they-- you know, there wasn't this dark cloud that hung over our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when did things start changing? When, you know-- did people seem to start to get more interested in learning about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was the-- the movie on television. Things started to be talked about on-- publicly, you know, on television, radio. Lena Kuchler wrote a book, which was published in 1961. My father noticed a little, little article in the that she was on a speaking tour in New York. And we went to see her. And she also had tried to get-- get it made into a TV movie. And finally, it was made. I think it was 1988, '89, something like that-- maybe '86-- it was on-- on television.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like, seeing her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was wonderful. It was wonderful. She-- when she got into Israel, she-- she had studied-- I think she had studied in Poland-- psychology. When she got into-- to Israel, she-- she was a psychologist. And she officiated at all the children's marriages and births. She was-- she was always very involved with all the children. At one point, I got an invitation to come. There was a reunion. But I never made it. But I visited her in New York-- twice actually. Two times I saw her-- that one time when she wrote the book and then again in-- many years later. And she died just prior to the-- to the-- to the film, the TV film being shown. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you end up in Plymouth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I came to school here. I was-- well, I came in 19-- in the '50s. I always liked Massachusetts. And then I wanted to be near the ocean. And we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is-- you know, looking back on-- on your experiences in your life, what do you think-- how do you think your experiences have-- have shaped or influenced who you are today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that it-- it made me strong. It made me self-sufficient. It-- I think it made me accepting of other people. One lesson that I got from my father was that he-- he was not an embittered person. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He-- he always felt that there were good people and bad people, and accepted people for who they are. And I think that's-- that was a very valuable lesson to me. And that's something that I've hopefully I've instilled in-- instilled in my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think that the war experiences--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I'm-- I'm very sad during holidays. I feel like I've been cheated of my childhood. I've been cheated of family. My kids don't have cousins. They don't-- you know, they don't have extended family because of that. And that's something that they have felt very strongly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your children get to know your father at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did, and they adored my father. In fact, my son, David, has written a book. And it's based on-- it's a novel, but it's based on my father's memoirs. He was very close to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your father pass away?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died in 1978.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. In-- in New Jersey?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Texas. He had open-heart surgery, and he died. He-- my son has often said that he-- he didn't fear death, because he was so close to death so many times that he-- he didn't fear it. You know, he was prepared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other words you'd like to add, being a survivor, any message you'd like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I speak about my experiences to groups-- children mostly. And-- and I-- I hope that when I speak to kids, I let them know that it's so important to speak up when they witness any kind of discrimination-- to be sensitive, and to-- don't stand by. Do-- you know-- be proactive. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My feelings toward Germans or Germany, I've-- I've had-- it-- it's taken me a long, long time to really be in touch with my feelings, you know. And it's sort of like peeling an onion. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I-- I have sort of mixed feelings. I've been-- I've sort of made a circle where I'm just at total acceptance. And now I'm-- I have a little-- a little fear and concern, and at times, a little discomfort with with Germany-- Germans or Germany. You know, it's like, I want to know, you know, what's your position, or what--

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have, yes. And the first time I went, it was horrendous. The second time I went, it was a little better. Same thing when I went to visit Poland. You know, when I-- first time I went, I didn't want to go to the museum. I didn't want to enjoy myself, you know. But I've met with my Polish family a couple times, and they're just so wonderful. They're very exceptional people. They-- they had a-- another brother-- uncle of these children-- who was a priest. He saved, I think, 200 Jews. I saw the book with all the Jewish names, because they made up baptismal papers for the-- for the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: He also hid a-- a Jewish person in the rectory and in the garden. So you know, they-- they're just a very special family. And-- and I-- and I consider them my family now, you know? I don't see them that often, but I call them, and I send packages. And they haven't been here. But they're dying. They're dying, you know? They're older than I am, so-- but now, our children know their children, which I hope they will continue to maintain contact. So. Is there anything else you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, there's a lot more, but-- Well, is there--

speaker: interviewee
text: --like, you know, like--

speaker: interviewer
text: --anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: --there's things about my father, you know, like how he survived, stories that he told.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Well, is there any particular-- I mean, this obviously is not, you know, your father's story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: But is there anything special that you would like to-- to say about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was an incredible person. He was just so kind and so good. But I think the war really affected his health, and he used to have terrible nightmares. I just remember him screaming at night, and you know, breaking out in cold sweat. And-- and I-- I know that it affected his heart and his health. When he died, he was only 73-- when he died. So-- he-- he was just a great person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I want to thank you very much for sharing your story. And-- thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
